coredata.dc:title: Heterointerface engineered and highly dual-functional N-doped carbon dot /N-rich g-C3N4 hybrid photocatalysts
coredata.dc:creator: Mohapatra, L.
Patra, D.
Zaidi, S.J.
Yoo, S.H.
coredata.dc:description: Coupled oxidation and reduction of 4-nitrophenol (4NP) and Cr(VI) offer unique advantages for wastewater treatment. In this study, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) were decorated on the surface of nitrogen-rich g-C3N4 (NCN) for simultaneous removal of 4NP and Cr (VI). The structural, optical, and electronic properties of the prepared catalysts were also investigated. The optimized 2.0% NCD/NCN catalyst exhibited excellent photocatalytic activity for the simultaneous removal of Cr(VI) (97%) and 4NP (95%) within 60 min under visible light by photogenerated electrons and holes, respectively. This superior photoactivity is attributed to the outstanding visible-light absorption, electron transferability, up-conversion photoluminescent properties and photosensitizing effect of NCDs. NCDs on the NCN surface improve the photocatalytic efficiency by minimizing undesired electron-hole recombination with a consequent increase in the lifetime of the heterostructure, as confirmed by photoluminescence, time resolved photoluminescence Spectroscopy, and Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. Additionally, the synergistic photocatalytic effect of the coexisting systems is due to the increasing electron traps of Cr(VI), and hole entraps of 4NP. To confirm the presence and contribution of the active species, free-radical quenching experiments, spectral analysis, and electron spin resonance measurements were performed. This unique OD/2D hybrid catalyst has enormous potential for effectively removing organic and inorganic pollutants from polluted industrial water without adding additional chemical reagents.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Coupled oxidation and reduction of 4-nitrophenol (4NP) and Cr(VI) offer unique advantages for wastewater treatment. In this study, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) were decorated on the surface of nitrogen-rich g-C3N4 (NCN) for simultaneous removal of 4NP and Cr (VI). The structural, optical, and electronic properties of the prepared catalysts were also investigated. The optimized 2.0% NCD/NCN catalyst exhibited excellent photocatalytic activity for the simultaneous removal of Cr(VI) (97%) and 4NP (95%) within 60 min under visible light by photogenerated electrons and holes, respectively. This superior photoactivity is attributed to the outstanding visible-light absorption, electron transferability, up-conversion photoluminescent properties and photosensitizing effect of NCDs. NCDs on the NCN surface improve the photocatalytic efficiency by minimizing undesired electron-hole recombination with a consequent increase in the lifetime of the heterostructure, as confirmed by photoluminescence, time resolved photoluminescence Spectroscopy, and Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. Additionally, the synergistic photocatalytic effect of the coexisting systems is due to the increasing electron traps of Cr(VI), and hole entraps of 4NP. To confirm the presence and contribution of the active species, free-radical quenching experiments, spectral analysis, and electron spin resonance measurements were performed. This unique OD/2D hybrid catalyst has enormous potential for effectively removing organic and inorganic pollutants from polluted industrial water without adding additional chemical reagents.

Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCD) on the surface of nitrogen-rich g -C3N4 (NCN) were designed for the simultaneous removal of organic and inorganic pollutants such as 4NP and Cr (VI).
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: The presence of organic or inorganic pollutants directly and secondarily introduced into the environment is a global concern because they are classified as carcinogens and pose a severe threat to human health. They are nondegradable and highly toxic at high concentrations. Among the organic pollutants, phenolics or derivatives of phenolic compounds, such as 4-nitrophenol (4NP), hydroquinone, and 4-chlorophenol, cause significant environmental problems [1]. Inorganic pollutants, such as Cr(VI), are typical heavy metal contaminants in industrial wastewater from various natural or anthropogenic sources, such as pest manufacturing companies, glass industries, wood preservative companies, and semiconductor production processes. Therefore, both organic and inorganic pollutants are highly toxic primary pollutants. In addition, in industrial wastewater, some phenolic compounds coexist with Cr(VI), significantly increasing the risk to the environment and human health [2,3]. Moreover, simultaneous treatment of these mixed contaminants is complicated. Therefore, a simple and effective technique to prevent contamination by both Cr(VI) and phenolic compounds is urgently needed. Nowadays, the photocatalytic redox process using a new multi-purpose photocatalyst is the most effective approach to removing pollutants in aquatic environments [4,5]. Some studies have shown that both phenol and Cr(VI) can be removed by the contact glow discharge electrolysis method [6]. Dielectric barrier plasma methods have also been used to simultaneously degrade phenolic contaminants and reduce Cr(VI) using catalysts [7]. These methods have demonstrated an excellent synergistic effect with adding Cr(VI), significantly enhancing the removal of phenolic contaminants. However, this leads to a secondary breakdown of the treatment liquid. Recent studies have reported the synergistic improvement of Cr(VI) reduction and RhB degradation by using RP/g-C3N4 and an RP/SnS2 photocatalyst owing to the quick utilization of electrons and holes by surface reactions. However, the simultaneous reduction of the heavy metal Cr(VI) and the decomposition of organic dyes have rarely been reported [8,9].

In recent years, photocatalytic technologies using visible/sunlight-active catalysts have attracted considerable attention because of their low cost and non-toxicity [10]. Among the different photocatalysts [11–18], g-C3N4 is a widely known metal-free photocatalyst owing to its various attractive features [19]. However, the photocatalytic efficiency of g-C3N4 is poor in practical applications because of its small surface area and high recombination rate, which limit photooxidation or reduction reactions. Several studies have been conducted to improve its photocatalytic activity, including doping, coating, and hybridization with other materials [20,21]. Among nonmetallic doping methods, nitrogen doping effectively enhances the photocatalytic performance by reducing bandgap energy, increasing electron mobility, and generating more active sites [22,23]. Recently, N-doped g-C3N4 (NCN) has been intensively studied for dye degradation and hydrogen evolution [24].

Carbon dots (CDs), a new type of zero-dimensional nanocarbon, exhibit excellent photoinduced electron transfer properties and can be used as a photocatalyst. To make CDs more effective, doping them with different heteroatoms improves their photoabsorbance properties and catalytic activity compared to that of undoped CDs [25]. Recent studies have shown that doping CQDs with NCDs can significantly affect their band structure and optoelectronic properties [26,27]. However, NCDs cannot be used as individual photocatalysts because they are electron mediators. In this regard, NCDs hybridized with any conductive material can accept photogenerated electrons from these materials and further enhance their photocatalytic activity. Hence, NCDs were combined with NCN nanoparticles to make the composite an ideal candidate for improving charge separation by hindering charge recombination, enhancing photocatalytic activity.

Therefore, in the present work, novel broad-spectrum light-driven NCD/NCN heterostructure-based photocatalysts were designed for the simultaneous removal of organic and inorganic pollutants such as 4NP and Cr(VI), respectively. The morphologies, compositions, and optical and electrochemical properties of the photocatalysts were systematically analyzed using several characterization methods. Furthermore, the results and proper mechanisms for enhancing the activity were studied. To the best of our knowledge, no studies have thoroughly investigated the simultaneous photocatalytic degradation of organic/inorganic pollutants and its mechanism using N-doped carbon dots and N-rich g-C3N4 hybrids.

Urea (CH4N2O, 99.5%), citric acid (CA, 99.5%), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37%), 4-Nitrophenol (4NP), potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7, 99.98%), and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were used as received from Sigma-Aldrich. Isopropyl alcohol (IPA, C3H8O, analytical reagent ≥98%), p-benzoquinone (BQ, ≥99%), sodium azide (NaN3, ≥99.5%), sodium oxalate (Na2C2O4 ≥99.5%), catalase (CAT), hydrogen peroxide solution (H2O2, 30%), and sulfuric acid were purchased from Merck. Double-distilled water was used in all photocatalytic experiments.

NCN catalysts were constructed using a previously reported method [24]. For comparison, a series of NCN photocatalysts with different N doping catalysts were prepared by changing the amount of citric acid monohydrate. The urea and citric acid monohydrate mass ratio was 2 g:1 mg, 2 g:2 mg, and 2 g:3 mg, and the samples were labeled as NCN21, NCN22, and NCN23, respectively. Pure g-C3N4 (CN) was prepared under the same conditions, except for citric acid monohydrate.

NCDs were synthesized using a previously reported method [28]. Briefly, the (3:1) citric acid to urea ratio was dissolved in 15 mL of DI water and stirred for 30 min. The mixture was then transferred to a Teflon-sealed stainless steel hydrothermal chamber and heated at 180 °C for 5 h. After natural cooling, large particles were removed by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 30 min. The suspension was dialyzed using a dialysis bag (molecular weight cut-off:10,000 Da) for 72 h, and the NCD powders were collected. Following this exact procedure, the CDs were synthesized using citric acid as the sole precursor.

The as-synthesized NCN22 powder (0.6 g) and a certain amount of NCDs (0.5, 1, 1.5, and 2.0%) were dispersed in 60 mL anhydrous ethanol by sonication for 1 h, followed by hydrothermal treatment at 140 °C for 4 h. Afterward, the obtained precipitate was centrifuged and washed with ethanol and DI water. Finally, the residue was dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C. The NCD/NCN materials were labeled 0.5NCD/NCN, 1NCD/NCN, 1.5NCD/NCN, and 2NCD/NCN, corresponding to the amount of NCDs (0.5%, 1%, 1.5%, and 2.0%, respectively). The synthetic strategy for the NCD/NCN system is illustrated in Fig. 1 . The same procedure was followed for synthesizing the different CD/NCN catalysts.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the as-prepared catalysts were analyzed using Cu Kα (0.15418 nm) by PANalytical (X’PERT-PRO Powder) installed at the Center for University-wide Research Facilities (CURF) at Jeonbuk National University and a Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer (XRD). The morphologies of the prepared catalysts were investigated using field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM; JSM 6701 F). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were captured on a JEOL-JEM-F200 electron microscope. The surface areas of the materials were measured using the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller method (BET) method (Micro metrics Instrument Corporation). A Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, PerkinElmer, USA) was used to analyze the catalysts. Diffuse reflectance ultraviolet–visible spectra (DRS) were recorded on a JASCO V-550 double-beam spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (LabRAM HR-800, Japan) excited at 365 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Multilab, Thermo, England). Total carbon analyzer data were collected using a TOC-L-CPH Shimadzu, Japan.

A batch test for the photocatalytic oxidation of 4NP and the reduction of Cr(VI) was conducted in a 30 mL aqueous solution of the target pollutant with 30 mg of the catalyst in light. HCl or NaOH solution was used to adjust the pH of the suspension. Before running the experiment, ultrasonic treatment was performed for 15 min to disperse the catalyst, followed by stirring for 60 min in the dark to facilitate the adsorption-desorption equilibrium. The simulated solar light source was visible light under a 100-W xenon lamp with a power density of 100 mW/cm2. A quartz reactor was used in all batch tests, and Cr (VI) in the solution was quantified colorimetrically using 1,5-diphenylcarbazide (DPC) [29]. A UV–Vis spectrophotometer was used to determine the total Cr(VI) concentration. The percentage of 4NP removal was analyzed using UV–vis spectroscopy by measuring the absorbance at 317 nm. The mineralization efficiency of 4NP was measured using a total organic carbon (TOC) analyzer. All photocatalytic experiments were performed in duplicate to confirm the reproducibility. To estimate the reusability of the photocatalyst, the used photocatalysts were filtered, collected, washed several times with DI water, dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C overnight, and used for the next run.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of superoxide and hydroxide radicals
ce:section.ce:para:
The electron spin resonance (ESR) method uses 5,5-dimethyl-l-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) to detect free radical species such as hydroxyl radicals (OH•) and superoxide radicals (
O 2
• −
). Specifically, to detect OH•, the catalyst was dispersed in DI water by ultrasonication to form a 2.5 mg/mL suspension. Then, 50 μL of DMPO was added to the suspension with rapid stirring, and the mixture was irradiated using a 100 W Xe lamp under fast stirring conditions. Subsequently, the supernatant of the mixture was rapidly tested using an ESR instrument. Similarly,
O 2
• −
was detected, except when the DI water was replaced with methanol.

The photocatalysts' photoelectrochemical (PEC) studies were performed in a three-electrode system on an electrochemical workstation (IVIUM n STAT). The light source was a 100 W xenon lamp with a UV light filter (400 nm). The calomel electrode, Pt wire, and prepared electrode served as the reference, counter, and working electrodes. 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution was used as the electrolyte in all experiments. For the working electrodes, 20 mg of the photocatalyst and iodine were dispersed in 30 mL acetone, followed by 10 min ultrasonication. Subsequently, a cleaned fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) glass substrate was coated with the dispersed photocatalyst using electrophoresis, dried at 70 °C, and used as the working electrode. Mott–Schottky (M − S) measurements were performed at 1000 Hz. Transient photocurrent measurements were conducted at a constant bias (0.5 V) to examine the steady-state photocurrent densities of the photoanodes. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed in the frequency range 0.05–105 Hz. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was conducted in the 0.05–100 Hz frequency range.

Free radical scavenging experiments can determine the roles of different typical reactive species in photocatalytic oxidation and reduction reactions. In this study, the scavengers used to trap the reactive species were IPA (10 mM), BQ (1.0 mM), Na2C2O4 (10 mM), NaN3 (75 mM), K2Cr2O7 (0.5 mM), and CAT, (0.07 mg/L). The catalyst dosage was 1 g/L, pH of the solution was 5.0, and 100 μM concentrations of Cr(VI) with 4NP were used separately or in a mixed form.

For the fabrication of nitrogen-doped g-C3N4, a lower amount of citric acid was added to melamine, implying reaction/condensation at 550 °C, which reduces the weight loss above the melting point of urea. Furthermore, the carboxyl groups of citric acid can react with the amine groups of urea, leading to the copolymerization of citric acid to form N-doped graphitic carbon. For the formation of NCD, melamine was reacted with citric acid under solid-state conditions. After the intermolecular and intramolecular polymerizations of the –COOH, –OH, and –NH2 groups, the activated intermediates were carbonized to form aromatic carbonic rings. N atoms were simultaneously incorporated as pyridine, pyrrolyl, and amide moieties into the heterocyclic ring system and were further condensed to form the final NCDs. Furthermore, the conjugated π-structure of NCDs interactivities with NCN results in a stable NCD/NCN hybrid system.

XRD patterns were used to determine the crystallographic structures of all the prepared catalysts. The two peaks at 13.2° (100) and 27.9° (002) in the XRD pattern of the NCN samples (Fig. 2 (a)) were ascribed to the in-plane packing of the tri-s-triazine units and interplane stacking of the aromatic structure, such as graphene, respectively [21]. Moreover, the (002) peaks of all the as-prepared NCN photocatalysts exhibit a slight shift toward a smaller angle with increasing citric acid concentration, which is attributed to the nitrogen dopant. In addition, the peaks corresponding to the NCN samples are slightly broader than those of CN, signifying the introduction of nitrogen into CN (inset in Fig. 2(a)) [23]. After loading the NCDs, no new peaks were evident as compared to pure NCN (Fig. 2(b)), demonstrating that the addition of NCDs does not change the molecular structure of pristine NCN because of the negligible crystallinity of the NCDs [30].

XPS was used to characterize the surface chemical compositions of the prepared catalysts. The XPS spectra in Fig. 3 confirm the coexistence of C, N, and O, and no other elements were observed. The C 1s high-resolution XPS spectra of CN centered at binding energies of 284.7 and 288.1 eV can be ascribed to C–C bonds corresponding to graphitized carbon bonded to the N atom (C–N bond) inside the triazine rings. The two peaks at 284.7 eV (25%) and 288.1 eV (75%) in NCN22 spectra differ from those (284.6 eV:42%, 288.8 eV:58%) of CN, which is further evidence of the nitrogen doping in NCN22 [31]. Additionally, an extra peak in the deconvoluted C 1s spectrum of 2NCD/NCN at approximately 285.7 eV (Fig. 3(a)) is ascribed to the C–OH bond in NCDs, confirming the loading of NCDs in NCN. This indicates that the π–π stacking interaction between NCN and NCDs is responsible for the formation of the NCD/NCN hybrid, which facilitates the formation of a high-quality contact interface between the two components without creating defects. The N 1s spectra of CN and NCN22 were fitted with four peaks (Fig. 3(b)). The first peak (398.8 eV) arose from the sp2-bonded N of the C–N=C triazine rings. The peaks at 399.9 and 401.87 eV are attributed to the N-(C)3 and C–N–H bonds, respectively, and the weak peak around 404.4 eV shows the π–π interactions. The N 1s spectra of all samples, CN, NCN22, and 2NCD/NCN, have typical tertiary N-(C)3 groups (399.9 eV) and triazine rings (398.8 eV) [29]. A notable shift of the nitrogen peaks toward lower binding energies confirms the interfacial interactions between the NCDs and NCN. Remarkably, there is a new shoulder peak at 397.8 eV evident for 2NCD/NCN, which may be attributed to the interaction of NCDs with NCN. Similarly, the O 1s peaks of pure CN, NCN22, and 2NCD/NCN have two peaks at 531.7 and 533.3 eV (Fig. 3(c)), which can be attributed to the O–C=O and C–OH/C–O–C bonds, respectively [32]. For CN and NCN22, the peaks at this position can be assigned to the absorbed water molecules from the air and the hydroxyl oxygen present on the catalyst surface. Notably, the peak intensity at 531.7 eV increases from 58.34% (NCN22) and 55.52% (CN) to 63.12% (2NCD/NCN) owing to the substantial presence of carboxyl groups in the NCDs. The intensity ratio of the peaks of (C–N=C)/(N–(C)3) in NCN22 (2.64) and 2NCD/NCN (2.61) was higher than that of CN (2.09), which indicates that the N atoms can substitute the C atoms in the bridging framework of CN. No noticeable change was observed in the case of 2NCD/NCN, confirming that NCDs were distributed on the surface of NCN22 (Table S1).

The FTIR spectra in Fig. 4 further confirm the presence of surface functional groups on the catalysts. The bands at approximately 3000 and 3600 cm−1 can be attributed to the N–H vibration due to the surface –NH2 functional groups and the O–H stretching mode from the surface-absorbed H2O, respectively. Additionally, the peaks from 1200 to 1700 cm–1 and 807 cm−1 belong to the typical stretching modes of the C–N and triazine ring modes of CN, respectively, which confirms that there is no noticeable change in the original graphitic C–N network after N doping. For 2NCD/NCN, a new bending vibration at 2918 cm−1 (C–H) was detected, and the peak at 1434 cm−1 became stronger, which could be ascribed to the combination of NCDs and NCN [33].

The nitrogen adsorption-desorption analysis results (Table S2) show that the surface area of CN changes slightly upon doping with nitrogen. It can be observed that the addition of a minimal amount of citric acid increases the surface area of CN slightly and makes the catalyst surface more porous. NCN22 shows the highest surface area among all the nitrogen-doped CN samples, which limits nitrogen doping in the CN framework. Alternatively, adding NCDs to the NCN surface decreases the surface area of the NCD/NCN catalysts and increases the amount of NCDs. The average pore size shows a decreasing trend with increasing amount of NCDs in the composites, which may be because the introduction of NCDs on the surface of NCN blocks the pores.

The morphologies of CN, NCN22, and 2NCD/NCN were observed in the TEM images, and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images are shown in Fig. 5 . CN typically exhibits large lamellar sheets with irregular porosity, whereas NCN22 and 2NCD/NCN exhibit more curved edges than CN [13]. Both CN and NCN22 exhibited layered graphite-like structures. It has been shown that NCDs with an average diameter of 7.6 nm are dispersed on the surface of the NCN. The HRTEM images of the NCDs confirm that the lattice spacing of 0.22 nm matches the (110) plane of graphite, and 0.326 nm can be attributed to the (002) plane of NCN. The HRTEM images showed that the negatively charged NCDs were directly attached to the NCN surface via a hydrothermal process.

The UV–Vis DRS spectra of all as-synthesized samples are shown in Fig. 6 (a). CN showed an absorption edge at ∼450 nm, reflecting its visible-light absorption properties. Additionally, N-doped CN extends the absorption edge owing to nitrogen's lone pair of electrons, prompting the delocalized π-electrons in the conjugated CN system. Fig. S1 shows the UV–vis absorption peaks of the NCDs solution at approximately 270 and 350 nm, which might be attributed to the π–π ∗ and n–π ∗ transitions of the nanocarbon particles. The emission signal originates from small sp2 domains [25]. Moreover, the NCD/NCN catalysts showed noticeably enhanced optical absorption, directly proving the presence of NCDs on the surface of NCN. This redshift is probably due to the photosensitizing effect of the NCDs. The occupied energy levels of the NCDs coupled with the energy levels of NCN resulted in a narrower bandgap in the hybrid systems. The bandgap energy (Eg) of the catalysts was calculated using the Kubelka-Munk theory from the plot of αhυ1/2 vs. hυ as shown in Fig. 6(b), where α, h, and υ represent the absorption coefficient, Planck's constant, and the light frequency, respectively [34]. The calculated Eg values for the catalysts are listed in Table S2. The NCD/NCN catalysts exhibited an absorption edge at a longer wavelength toward visible light than that of the NCN22 catalyst, and the lowest bandgap energy was recorded for the 2NCD/NCN catalyst. The enhanced optical absorption properties and decreased Eg can ease the capture of visible light photons and are favorable for improving photocatalytic activity.

To better understand the energy band structures of CN, NCN22, and 2NCD/NCN, the conduction band minimum (CBM) and valence band maximum (VBM) were obtained from electrochemical mass spectroscopy (MS) and Tauc plots. The flat-band potentials (Vfb) were estimated from the M − S plots (1/C2 vs. potential) using the following equation:
(1)
1 /
C 2
=
[
( 2 / e
ε 0
rN d
A 2
)
(
− V +
V fb
− kT / e
)
]
where C is the differential capacitance, ε 0 is the permittivity of free space, ε is the dielectric constant of the semiconductor, Nd represents the density of dopants, A is the area, Vfb is the flat-band potential, V is the applied potential, k is the Boltzmann constant, T is the absolute temperature, and e is the elementary charge [35]. The flat-band potentials of the CN, NCN22, and 2NCD/NCN catalysts were obtained from the x-intercept (x = 0) of the linear region of the 1/C2 vs. potential curves (Fig. 7 (a)). The calculated Vfb values for CN, NCN22, and 2NCD/NCN are −0.69, −0.60, and −0.58 V, respectively, vs. the standard hydrogen electrode (SCE), indicating n-type semiconductors, and are −0.49, −0.36, and −0.34 V, respectively, vs. normal hydrogen electrode (NHE). The corresponding valence band (VB) potentials obtained from the band gap energy are +2.16, +1.99, and +1.65 V, respectively, vs. NHE. As shown in Fig. 7(b), the VB edge potential is higher, and the conduction band (CB) edge potential is lower for 2NCD/NCN than in NCN22, leading to a reduced band gap energy (Eg) of 2NCD/NCN.

Photocurrent measurements and EIS were performed to observe the electron generation and charge-transfer kinetics of the prepared catalysts. The transient photocurrent curves with on-off cycles arising from the chronoamperometric current vs. time (I-t) curves are shown in Fig. 7(c). Pure CN exhibited the lowest photocurrent response, owing to its poor electrical conductivity. The addition of nitrogen doping (NCN22) enhanced the current density to 2.5 μA/cm2, and the highest photocurrent response was observed for the 2NCD/NCN catalyst (3 μA/cm2), which was more than twice that of CN (1.4 μA/cm2). The higher photocurrent density exhibited by the 2NCD/NCN catalyst resulted in enhanced charge-carrier separation efficiency and photogeneration of more electrons, consequently boosting the redox performance. Furthermore, the results were supported by the Nyquist plots obtained from the EIS measurements, as shown in Fig. 7(d). Generally, the smallest arc radius indicates the highest charge carrier separation efficiency of the system. The smaller arc radius for the 2NCD/NCN catalyst than the other catalysts suggests a reduction in the electron-transfer resistance, leading to a fast charge carrier separation rate, which is consistent with the PL (discussed later). Consequently, the above results demonstrate that NCD promotes the separation and transport of photogenerated charge carriers on the NCN surface to enhance the photocatalytic activity.

As shown in Fig. 7(e), the main emission peak of CN is positioned at approximately 450 nm. It can be observed that the PL intensity of NCN22 is significantly lower than that of CN, NCN21, and NCN23, implying that the recombination behavior is suppressed substantially due to the introduction of the nitrogen atom, thus improving the charge separation. Similarly, the PL intensity of the hybrid catalyst is considerably lower, indicating that the presence of NCD in the hybrid system significantly hinders the electron-hole recombination rate. Furthermore, the NCD/NCN catalysts exhibit an apparent redshift in the emission peak from 450 to 455 nm. The peak at 455 nm in the 2NCD/NCN spectrum is noticeably weaker than those of the other NCD/NCN catalysts, which, similar to a previous report, implies a remarkable improvement in electron and hole separation because of the presence of more NCDs on the NCN surface. Furthermore, the upconversion photoluminescence properties of NCDs may effectively reduce the recombination of charge carriers indicating the important sensitization role of NCDs in NCN surface [32]. Additionally, emission lifetime measurements were performed using time-resolved PL (TRPL). The emission decay data were analyzed using a bi-exponential kinetics model (Equation (2)), from which the decay components were derived [21]. The TRPL spectra and calculated lifetimes of the photogenerated charges in CN, NCN22, and 2NCD/NCN are presented in Fig. 7(f) and Table 1 , respectively.
(2) I(t) = A1 exp (t/τ1) + A2 exp (t/τ2)
Where ‘I’ represents the normalized emission intensity, τ1 and τ2 represent the decay times for the faster and slower components, respectively; and A1 and A2 are the amplitudes of the components.

The average lifetime (τav) is calculated as follows:
(3)

As shown in Table 1, the average lifetime of the photogenerated charge carriers in the 2NCD/NCN composite was 46 ns which was 2.7 and 2.1 times longer than those of CN and NCN22, respectively. The decrease in radiative recombination can explain the increase in the photoluminescence lifetime. The remarkable quenching of the PL signal and prolonged excited charge carriers on the surface demonstrate the excellent photocatalytic activity of the hybrid catalysts.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic reactions in a single pollutant system
ce:section.ce:para:
First, photocatalytic oxidation and reduction of 4NP and Cr(VI) were accomplished separately at pH 5 using CN, NCN21, NCN22, NCN23, 0.5NCD/NCN, 1NCD/NCN, 1.5NCD/NCN, and 2NCD/NCN at a catalyst dose of 1 g/L. Before starting the photocatalytic experiment, the aqueous pollutant with the catalysts was treated without light to reach adsorption-desorption equilibria. The adsorption of both 4NP and Cr(VI) did not yield any noticeable results (Fig. S2(a)). Photocatalytic experiments were performed, and the results are shown in Fig. S2(b). To compare the effect of nitrogen doping on the carbon dot hybrid materials, catalysts such as 0.5CD/NCN, 1CD/NCN, 1.5CD/NCN, and 2CD/NCN were tested in a single system. The photocatalytic 4NP oxidation of CN, NCN21, NCN22, and NCN23 reached approximately 25%, 52%, 71%, and 65%, respectively, after 120 min of visible light irradiation. Under identical experimental conditions, 30%, 55%, 75%, and 72% of Cr(VI) was removed by CN, NCN21, NCN22, and NCN23, NCN22 showed the highest removal rates for both 4NP and Cr(VI), which might be due to the increased surface area. If CN is compared with NCN, N atom doping is responsible for the extension and delocalization of aromatic π conjugated systems and helps the separation and transfer of charge carriers to enhance photocatalytic activity. Moreover, the photocatalytic efficiency increased significantly from 71% to 84% for 4NP degradation and 75%–86% for Cr(VI) reduction by introducing NCDs, 71%–77% for 4NP degradation, and 75%–79% for Cr(VI) reduction using CD/NCNs from 0 to 2 wt% over the same timeline (180 min) under visible light irradiation. In order to provide a comprehensive comparison of the photocatalytic properties of NCDs/NCN with CDs/NCN, the NCD hybrid catalysts showed better results than CD hybrid catalysts as shown in Fig.S2 (b & C). The experimental data were fitted to a first-order kinetic equation as follows:
(4) ln (Ct/C0) = −Kappt
Where Kapp is the apparent rate constant, C0 is the initial concentration of the solution, t is the reaction time, and Ct is the solution concentration at the time of the reaction [36]. From the rate constants of the different catalysts (Table 2 ), it can be observed that NCN22 showed enhanced activity compared to the other N-doped CN catalysts. However, compared to the photocatalytic activity of all the NCD/NCN catalysts, 2NCD/NCN showed the highest rate constant value (0.087 min−1 for Cr(VI) and 0.075 min−1 for 4NP). Moreover, 2NCD/NCN showed slightly enhanced photocatalytic activity compared to the 1.5 NCD/NCN and 2NCD/NCN catalysts. Therefore, the further addition of NCDs to NCN reduces the formation of reactive species due to the excess amounts of NCDs starting the competition with NCN via the absorption of photons. Therefore, it can be concluded that an optimum loading of 2% NCD in NCN22 (2NCD/NCN) can effectively enhance the photoactivity in both cases. Compared with the CD/NCN hybrid catalysts, the NCD/NCN photocatalysts showed better activity (Table S4), which might be due to the nitrogen dopant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synergistic effect of 2NCD/NCN catalyst on Cr(VI) reduction and 4NP oxidation
ce:section.ce:para: Next, the simultaneous photooxidation of 4NP and the reduction of Cr(VI) were investigated. First, the simultaneous photocatalytic redox reactions of 4NP and Cr(VI) without a catalyst were studied, and no proper redox reactions were observed between 4NP and Cr(VI) (Fig. S3(a)). Subsequently, photocatalytic reactions were performed using the catalysts under dark conditions to investigate the adsorption-desorption equilibrium of 4NP and Cr(VI). The adsorption of both 4NP and Cr(VI) onto 2NCD/NCN was similar to that of the single system, indicating that 4NP did not interact with Cr(VI) (Fig. S3(b)).

Fig. 8 (a) and (b) show the effect of pH (ranging from 3 to 9) on the simultaneous removal of Cr(VI) and 4NP, respectively, using the 2NCD/NCN catalyst. Apparently, the effect of 4NP on Cr(VI) removal is more evident in pH 5 than in acidic solution. However, Cr(VI) reduction proceeds faster under acidic conditions owing to electrostatic interactions [37], which can be explained by the fact that, in the mixed solution, the pH of the solution did not change as the concentration of hydroxyl radicals decreased because of the presence of 4NP. Consequently, the in-situ formation of H2O2 was inhibited, which helped reduce Cr(VI) to Cr (III). Moreover, at pH > 6, the major species in Cr(VI) is
Cr
O 4
2 -
, and the oxidation potential of HCrO4 − was higher (E0(HCr
O 4
-
/Cr3+) = 0.94 V, acidic pH) than that of
Cr
O 4
2 -
(E0(
Cr
O 4
2 -
/Cr (OH)3 = 0.56 V, neutral pH), which indicates that Cr(VI) is more easily reduced in acidic than neutral or alkaline conditions. Moreover, with an increase in the initial pH, a slight decrease in the removal efficiency of 4NP was observed. Therefore, it can be concluded that the optimal conditions for the present study were pH 5. The details of the rate constants at different pH values are shown in Fig. 8(a) and (b).

Fig. 8(c) shows the effect of varying the concentrations of 4NP and Cr(VI) on the photocatalytic reactions in the 4NP/Cr(VI) coexisting system. With an initial concentration of 100 μM 4NP, the removal efficiencies were 87%, 95%, and 85% in the presence of 50, 100, and 150 μM Cr(VI), respectively, indicating that the degradation of 4NP increased with increasing concentration of Cr(VI) up to 100 μM and then decreased significantly. However, the reduction efficiencies of 100 μM Cr(VI) were 89%, 97%, and 98% for 4NP concentrations of 50, 100, and 150 μM, respectively. This is possible because Cr(VI) competes with the active species of 4NP, and Cr(VI) possesses advantages in obtaining more active species. However, the reduction of Cr(VI) was approximately 99% when the concentration of 4NP was 150 μM, whereas it was only approximately 78% without 4NP, which acted as a hole scavenger. It can be observed that there is no significant change in Cr(VI) reduction in the presence of 100 and 150 μM 4NP.

Although the degradation rate of 4NP decreased with increasing concentrations of Cr(VI) by more than 100 μM in the coexisting system, by trapping the photogenerated holes and acting as an electron donor in the valence band, the reduction efficiency of Cr(VI) was enhanced. Without the hole scavenger (4NP), the Cr(VI) reduction rate was very low. At low concentrations (50 μM), 4NP did not efficiently scavenge VB holes. Therefore, approximately 95% of 4NP and 97% of Cr(VI) were reduced within 60 min in the coexisting system at a concentration of 100 μM. This suggests a method for efficient simultaneous removal of the two pollutants from contaminated water without using additional electron/hole scavengers. As Fig. S2(b) shows, Cr(VI) was reduced alone, the system proceeded slowly, and approximately 85% could be reduced within 180 min. However, in the coexisting system, this process was significantly enhanced within 60 min. Tables 3 (a) and 3(b) list the detailed rate constants.

Further, the photocatalytic activity of 4NP oxidation and Cr(VI) reduction was investigated with varying catalyst doses ranging from 0.5 g/L to 1.5 g/L (Fig. 8(d)). By keeping the initial concentrations of 4NP and Cr(VI) constant at 100 μM each in the coexisting system and increasing the catalyst doses from 0.5 g/L to 1.0 g/L, faster removal efficiency was achieved. Further increasing the catalyst concentration reduced the efficiency slightly in both cases. This result demonstrates that when the catalyst amount is 0.5 g/L, the active species formed by the photocatalytic processes are insufficient to reduce or oxidize the pollutants. Additionally, when the dose of the photocatalyst is greater than 1 g/L, the particles block photon transmission and reduce the formation of charge carriers and active species, thus decreasing the removal efficiency. Therefore, the optimum catalyst dose was 1 g/L.

The percentage of mineralization of 4NP in the presence of 100 M Cr (VI) was also assessed by measuring the TOC, as shown in Fig. 8(e). After 60 min of irradiation, 91.5% of the 4NP was decomposed, whereas 82% of the TOC was removed. Although the removal percentage was higher, the results indicated that some more stable products might still be present in the system. However, ∼87% of the TOC was removed after 180 min of irradiation, indicating that 4NP was completely mineralized using 2NCD/NCN.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Durability of 2NCD/NCN for simultaneous removal of 4NP and Cr(VI)
ce:section.ce:para: The photostability and recyclability of photocatalysts are crucial for their practical application. Therefore, to estimate the photostability of the 2NCD/NCN catalyst, its photoactivity was evaluated through five successive experiments with catalyst recycling in the coexisting system (100 μM concentration of Cr(VI) and 4NP). After each run, the used catalyst was separated from the reactor, washed in distilled water, and dried at 80 °C. As shown in Fig. 9 (a) and (b), there was no significant change in the photoactivity for 4NP oxidation or Cr(VI) reduction for up to three consecutive cycles. After four cycles, the removal efficiencies of 4NP and Cr(VI) reached 85% and 80%, respectively, for simultaneous photoredox reactions. In addition, the XRD pattern of the photocatalyst (Fig. 9(c)) did not change significantly after three cycles. In the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) plot (Fig. 9(d)), the noticeable peaks at band energies of 586.8, 577.1, and 579.0 eV for the used 2NCD/NCN correspond to Cr(III) 2p3/2, Cr(III) 2p1/2, and Cr(VI), respectively, which show that the presence of Cr is 23% for Cr(VI) and 78% for Cr(III) on the surface of the catalyst, indicating that most of the Cr(VI) is reduced to the less toxic Cr(III). The XPS results confirmed that the Cr (III) produced could precipitate on the surface. Moreover, the decrease in photoactivity after four cycles may be due to Cr(III) adsorption onto the catalyst surface or weight reduction during the recycling process. These results reveal that the hybrid catalyst is stable and reusable for pollutant removal under visible light for up to four successive cycles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reactive species involved and their role
ce:section.ce:para:
To elucidate the combined pollutant removal mechanism of the 2NCD/NCN, NCN22, and CN catalysts, ESR experiments were performed using DMPO as the spin-trapping species. As shown in Fig. 10 (a) and (b), four-line ESR signals (1:1:1:1 and 1:2:2:1) were detected, which are characteristic of DMPO-OH• and DMPO-
O 2
• −
, respectively. Additionally, the 2NCD/NCN catalyst displayed better signals for both DMPO-OH• and DMPO-
O 2
• −
than NCN22 and CN, suggesting that additional OH• and
O 2
• −
might be produced after introducing NCD on the NCN22 surface. However, the more negative valance band potential of the photogenerated holes [2NCD/NCN (EVB = +1.26 V vs. NHE)] than OH• radicals [(E (•OH/OH−) = +1.99 V vs. NHE) and E (OH·/H2O) = +2.27 eV vs. NHE)] indicates that the valence band holes are incapable of oxidizing hydroxyl groups into OH• radicals. Similarly, the photogenerated electrons on the CB of 2NCD/NCN combine with atmospheric oxygen to generate O2 •-, and the CB potential of 2NCD/NCN is −0.34 eV vs. NHE is slightly more negative than E0(O2/
O 2
• −
) = −0.33 eV vs. NHE.

In addition, as the n-type dopant (N atom) has a strong electron affinity, the electrons in the CB of NCN quickly transfer to the NCDs and create separation of charge carriers. Subsequently, the electrons of the NCDs might be trapped by molecular O2, resulting in
O 2
• −
which could react with holes to yield 1O2. Subsequently,
O 2
• −
reacts with water to form H2O2. Therefore, it was verified that OH• radicals were generated from H2O2 via photochemical reactions. Additionally, two superoxide molecules can be converted into one molecule of H2O2. Significantly, the highest intensity of the ESR signal of 2NCDs/NCN implies higher concentrations of OH• and
O 2
• −
than NCN22 and CN, which is consistent with the observed photoactivity results in the coexisting system (simultaneous oxidation and reduction processes). Table S3 shows the reported performance of various carbon and carbon-based reactive groups introduced into the g-C3N4 semiconductor to remove various pollutants according to prominent dominant factors. Carbon materials with g-C3N4 composites have been diversified to enhance their synergetic properties, providing a complete understanding of the carbon-induced increase in the rate of pollutant decomposition.

To validate the effect of the contribution of various reactive species, such as OH•,
O 2
• −
, photogenerated holes (h+), singlet oxygen (1O2), photogenerated electrons (e−), and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), on the simultaneous photooxidation and reduction reactions of the 2NCD/NCN catalyst, radical scavenging experiments were carried out in the presence of IPA, BQ, Na2C2O4, NaN3, K2Cr2O7, and CAT. It can be observed in Figs. S4(a) and S4(b), K2Cr2O7 exhibited noticeable inhibitory effects toward the reduction of Cr(VI), with an inhibition rate of 95%. Meanwhile, with the addition of IPA, BQ, and CAT, the reduction rate decreased slightly when OH•,
O 2
• −
, and H2O2 were trapped, suggesting that e− from the CB was favorably involved in the photocatalytic removal of Cr(VI), and OH•,
O 2
• −
, and H2O2 had a minor effect on Cr(VI) removal. The production of H2O2 in the 2NCD/NCN system could be the role of NCDs and that of the OH• radicals via homolytic cleavage in a secondary reaction pathway (Equation (8)). As the standard electrode potential of H2O2 is lower than that of Cr(VI) at an acidic pH, and by the in-situ production of H2O2, Cr(VI) can be converted to Cr(III) [38]. Moreover, in the presence of bubbling of N2 gas, the Cr(VI) removal efficiency was slightly enhanced, which was ascribed to the diminished electron supply to O2, demonstrating the role of e− in this system. IPA, Na2C2O4, CAT, and NaN3 exhibit inhibition by 45%, 78%, 25%, and 19.5%, respectively, for 4NP oxidation, confirming that h+, OH•, and H2O2 directly participate in the oxidation of 4NP, whereas the effects of 1O2 and N2 are not significant. In conclusion, all the radicals described above play an essential role in simultaneous redox reactions. The calculated inhibition rates (%) of different scavengers are listed in Table 4 .

The formation of OH• radicals on the catalyst's surface was further confirmed by fluorescence spectroscopy using terephthalic acid (TA) as a chemical probe for OH• radical detection. A solution containing TA was excited at λex = 315 nm (Fig. S4(c)). The fluorescence signal at λex = 425 nm indicates the generation of OH• radicals. The fluorescence intensity was higher for 2NCD/NCN than for NCN22 and CN, which is expected to form more OH• for the photocatalytic system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Role of NCDs in NCN for simultaneous photocatalytic reaction
ce:section.ce:para: From the above experimental data, the enhanced photocatalytic efficiency of the 2NCD/NCN hybrid system may be derived from the synergistic effect of the NCDs on the NCN22 catalyst surface by creating an interface. Therefore, M − S analysis was used to evaluate the flat-band potential (Vfb) of the catalyst. Fig. 7(b) shows the detailed band structures of CN, NCN22, and 2NCD/NCN. The bottom of the CB and top of the VB of the pristine CN, NCN22, are −0.49 eV, +2.16 eV, and −0.36 eV, +1.99 eV, respectively. Nevertheless, after NCDs are introduced on the surface of NCN22 (2NCD/NCN), the CB edge potential is −0.34 eV, which is lower than that of NCN22, and the VB edge potential is +1.65 eV, which is higher than that of NCN22, resulting in a narrow bandgap (Fig. 11 ). Therefore, the extent of the formation of photoexcited charge carriers improves the utilization efficiency of visible light.

Moreover, by introducing NCDs on NCN, Eg was reduced from 2.34 to 1.99 eV. This indicates that intrinsic charges are generated in the 2NCD/NCN hybrid catalyst owing to excited electron injection from the VB to the CB in NCN. Because of the change in the absorption edge of the hybrid system, it can be assumed that NCDs display their upconversion photoluminescence properties as well as acted as electron transfer channels. Additionally, the Fermi level of NCDs (−0.35 eV) was significantly lower than that of NCN (−0.36 eV), thus proving that electron transfer from NCN to NCDs can occur quickly. Subsequently, NCDs can act as electron reservoirs and donors in the photocatalytic processes. They can efficiently scavenge electrons from the CB of NCN and support a reduction reaction. Hence, it was assumed that photogenerated electrons were transferred from the CB of NCN22 to that of the NCDs. In contrast, the O2 adsorbed catalyst captures electrons from the CB of NCN to form O2 •- because the CB of NCN (−0.36 eV vs. NHE) has a more negative potential than O2/
O 2
• −
(−0.33 eV vs. NHE). However, as the VB of NCN (+1.99 eV vs. NHE) is less positive than the potential of •OH/OH− (2.38 eV vs. NHE), the holes on the VB of NCN cannot form OH•. In addition, the photoinduced holes of NCN cannot oxidize the adsorbed H2O molecules to generate •OH because the VB potential is smaller than E0(H2O/OH·) = +2.4 eV vs. NHE. The formation of OH• radicals was due to the photochemical reactions of H2O2 (discussed in Section 3.3.1). Subsequently, the photogenerated CB electrons could directly reduce Cr(VI) to Cr(III) (Cr(VI)/Cr(III) = 0.51 eV) and produce O2 to O2 •-. Furthermore, 4NP can be oxidized by photogenerated holes. The detailed mechanism are described in Equations (5)−(11). In the 2NCD/NCN photocatalytic reaction system, Cr(VI) and 4NP acted as electron acceptors and donors, respectively, reacting with the photogenerated CB electrons and photoexcited holes. This process can promote the separation of charge carriers and subsequently form more CB electrons for Cr(VI) reduction and VB holes for 4NP oxidation. Because organic compounds are good hole scavengers and are known to accelerate the reduction process, 4NP is supposed to act as a hole scavenger to improve the reduction rate of Cr(VI) in our study. When light is irradiated, the holes are scavenged by 4NP, accelerating the reduction of Cr(VI). Thus, both pollutants were simultaneously and effectively removed. Compared with CDs/NCN, the NCD/NCN catalysts can provide excess electrons to eject into the carbon dot, alter the electronic and optical properties, and enhance the photocatalytic activity.

Proposed pathway for the degradation of 4NP.
Image 1

This study demonstrated the simultaneous 4NP oxidation and Cr(VI) reduction by investigating its removal under visible-light illumination. Although several studies have been conducted on the photoreduction of Cr(VI) in the presence of organic compounds (such as methanol, ethanol, citric acid, and formic acid) as hole scavengers, this study presents an efficient way to degrade the two pollutants simultaneously without any additional chemical reagents.

In this study, novel visible-light-induced NCD/NCN photocatalysts were designed and studied for single and simultaneous removal of Cr(VI) and 4NP. The optimized 2NCD/NCN showed a significant photocatalytic removal efficiency for simultaneous Cr(VI) reduction (97%) and 4NP oxidation (96%) after 60 min of light irradiation. The enhanced photocatalytic properties of the optimum hybrid are due to the synergistic effects of reduced charge carrier recombination rates, increased transient photocurrent response owing to increased charge density to promote rapid charge transfer, and improved optical properties due to the incorporation of NCDs, and superior up-conversion photoluminescence property which are beneficial for improving photocatalytic performance. In addition, NCDs behave as electron reservoirs and reduce the recombination of charge carriers. Furthermore, the enhanced photocatalytic efficiency in the mixed pollutant system is attributed to the dual functions of 4NP oxidation and Cr(VI) reduction, leading to the efficient separation of charge carriers and the generation of more electrons and holes. In this regard, the novelty of this work is the simultaneous minimization of organic-inorganic waste and energy consumption. Moreover, organic compounds also benefit from serving as electron donors when used to reduce inorganic pollutants such as Cr(VI) during photocatalytic reactions. This work proposes a new promising route towards designing novel metal-free photocatalysts to address various environmental problems, which are the biggest challenges faced by modern society today.

Seung Hwa Yoo: supervision, funding acquisition, investigation, review, and editing of the original draft.
